title: Kalori Açığı Hesaplayıcısı — Güvenli Kilo Kaybı Planı
description: Kalori açığınızı ve tahmini kilo kaybı sürenizi hesaplayın. Hedef kilonuza ulaşmak için haftalık ve günlük kalori hedefleri.

faq.title: Frequently Asked Questions
faq.q1: How is the 7,700 kcal per kilogram figure derived?
faq.a1: The 7,700 kcal/kg figure is based on the energy density of human adipose (fat) tissue. One kilogram of body fat contains approximately 7,700 kilocalories of stored energy — derived from the caloric density of fat (9 kcal/g), adjusted for the water content of adipose tissue (approximately 15% water by mass). The widely cited US figure of 3,500 kcal per pound converts to 7,716 kcal/kg. In practice, actual fat loss per deficit kilocalorie varies due to individual metabolic differences, so this figure is a well-validated population average, not a precise individual prediction.
faq.q2: Should my daily calorie target ever go below my BMR?
faq.a2: No. Eating below BMR — the calories your body needs to sustain vital functions at complete rest — is clinically unsafe for sustained periods and counterproductive for long-term weight loss. Severe restriction below BMR triggers aggressive metabolic adaptation (the body reduces metabolic rate by 15–30%), causes significant muscle loss (up to 50% of weight lost may be muscle at very low intakes), increases cortisol (causing fat storage), and leads to nutritional deficiencies. The safest minimum is 1,200 kcal/day for most women and 1,500 kcal/day for most men — and these levels should only be maintained short-term with medical supervision.
faq.q3: What is the maximum safe weekly weight loss rate?
faq.a3: The CDC and NIH recommend 0.5–1.0 kg (1–2 lbs) per week as the safe, sustainable rate for most adults. At 0.5 kg/week, you preserve more muscle mass, experience less metabolic adaptation, have better dietary adherence, and are more likely to maintain the loss long-term. At 1.0 kg/week or above, you may lose significant muscle alongside fat, especially without resistance training and adequate protein. Weight loss above 1.5 kg/week in non-obese individuals almost always includes substantial muscle loss.
faq.q4: Why does weight loss stall even when I maintain my calorie deficit?
faq.a4: Weight loss stalls are caused by three main factors. First, as you lose weight, your TDEE decreases — a lighter body requires fewer calories. If you do not recalculate your deficit as weight drops, your original deficit gradually becomes smaller. Second, metabolic adaptation: the body reduces metabolic rate by 5–15% in response to sustained calorie restriction, narrowing the deficit further. Third, water retention fluctuations can mask fat loss on the scale for 1–3 weeks. If weight has truly stalled for more than 3 weeks, recalculate your TDEE at your new body weight and reduce intake by 100–200 kcal/day.
faq.q5: What authority recommends specific calorie deficit targets?
faq.a5: The Centers for Disease Control and Prevention (CDC) and National Institutes of Health (NIH) recommend a 500–1,000 kcal/day deficit for a loss of 0.5–1.0 kg/week. The Academy of Nutrition and Dietetics recommends never going below 1,200 kcal/day for women or 1,500 kcal/day for men without medical supervision. The European Society for Clinical Nutrition and Metabolism (ESPEN) recommends protein intake of at least 1.2 g/kg body weight during weight loss to preserve lean mass.
faq.q6: How does protein intake affect calorie deficit results?
faq.a6: Protein is the most important macronutrient during a calorie deficit. It has the highest thermic effect (25–30% of calories are burned in digestion vs 5–10% for carbs and 0–3% for fat), preserves lean muscle mass during weight loss, and increases satiety more than carbohydrates or fat. Research consistently shows that increasing protein to 1.6–2.4 g/kg body weight during a deficit results in more fat loss and less muscle loss compared to lower protein intakes at the same calorie level. Aim for 25–40 g of protein per meal for optimal muscle protein synthesis.
faq.q7: What is metabolic adaptation and how do I combat it?
faq.a7: Metabolic adaptation (also called adaptive thermogenesis) is the body's response to calorie restriction: it reduces BMR by 5–30%, decreases NEAT (unconscious movement), slows digestion, and reduces the thermic effect of food. Over time, this narrows and eventually eliminates your calorie deficit even without changing intake. Strategies that mitigate metabolic adaptation: resistance training (preserves metabolic-rate-supporting muscle mass), diet breaks (2-week periods at TDEE between deficit phases), refeeds (1–2 days per week at or slightly above TDEE), and cycling between deficit and maintenance phases rather than continuous restriction.
faq.q8: Can I create a calorie deficit purely through exercise without dieting?
faq.a8: Theoretically yes, but practically difficult. To create a 500 kcal/day deficit through exercise alone requires 60–90 minutes of moderate-intensity activity daily — a substantial time commitment. Additionally, exercise tends to increase appetite proportionally, often leading to compensatory eating that partially or fully offsets the exercise-created deficit. Research (including work by Herman Pontzer at Duke University) has shown that total daily energy expenditure from exercise is partially constrained by metabolic compensation. Combining a modest dietary deficit (300 kcal) with exercise (200 kcal) is more sustainable and effective than either approach alone.

content.whatIs:
Kalori açığı hesaplayıcı, seçilen bir oranda kilo verme hedefinize ulaşmak için gereken günlük kalori alımını belirler. TDEE'nizden (Toplam Günlük Enerji Harcaması — mevcut aktivite düzeyinizde vücudunuzun her gün yaktığı kaloriler) başlar ve seçtiğiniz oranda vücut yağını kaybetmek için yeterli enerji açığı oluşturmak için gereken azalmayı hesaplar.

Kalori açığı, vücudunuzun yediklerinizden ihtiyaç duyduğu tüm enerjiyi bulamadığında ortaya çıkar. Vücut, gıdadan ihtiyaç duyduğu tüm enerjiyi bulamadığında, farkı kapatmak için depolanmış enerjiye — başta vücut yağı — başvurur. Bir kilogram vücut yağı yaklaşık 7.700 kcal depolanmış enerji içerir. Bu nedenle, sürekli 550 kcal'lik günlük açık, haftada yaklaşık 0,5 kg vücut yağını tüketir. Termodinamiğe dayanan bu enerji dengesi ilkesi, kullanılan spesifik diyet çerçevesinden bağımsız olarak her kanıta dayalı kilo verme yaklaşımının temelidir.

Bu hesaplayıcı, sürdürülebilir kilo verme planlaması için CDC, NIH ve DSÖ tarafından önerilen 7.700 kcal/kg denklemini kullanır. Günlük kalori hedefinizi, hedef kiloya ulaşmak için gereken toplam açığı ve gerçekçi bir zaman çizelgesini sağlar — genel bir hedef yerine eksiksiz, bilime dayalı bir plan sunar.
content.formula:
Günlük Açık = (haftalık kayıp hedefi × 7.700) ÷ 7

Burada 7.700 kcal ≈ 1 kg vücut yağında depolanan enerji

Günlük Kalori Hedefi = TDEE − Günlük Açık
Gereken Toplam Açık = (mevcut kilo − hedef kilo) × 7.700
Hedefe Ulaşma Haftaları = (mevcut kilo − hedef kilo) ÷ haftalık kayıp hedefi

Güvenli Aralık Kontrolü:
  Minimum günlük alım: 1.200 kcal (kadınlar) / 1.500 kcal (erkekler)
  Maksimum önerilen haftalık kayıp: 1,0 kg/hafta (obez olmayan bireyler için)
content.example:
Örnek 1 — Orta açık, kadın:
Mevcut: 75 kg | Hedef: 65 kg | TDEE: 2.100 kcal | Hedef: 0,5 kg/hafta
Günlük açık = (0,5 × 7.700) ÷ 7 = 550 kcal
Günlük hedef: 2.100 − 550 = 1.550 kcal
Gereken toplam açık: 10 × 7.700 = 77.000 kcal
Zaman çizelgesi: 10 ÷ 0,5 = 20 hafta (~5 ay)

Örnek 2 — Hafif açık, erkek:
Mevcut: 90 kg | Hedef: 82 kg | TDEE: 2.800 kcal | Hedef: 0,25 kg/hafta
Günlük açık = (0,25 × 7.700) ÷ 7 = 275 kcal
Günlük hedef: 2.800 − 275 = 2.525 kcal
Zaman çizelgesi: 8 ÷ 0,25 = 32 hafta — ama çok rahat ve kası koruyucu

Örnek 3 — Agresif açık (maksimum önerilen):
Mevcut: 95 kg | Hedef: 80 kg | TDEE: 2.900 kcal | Hedef: 1,0 kg/hafta
Günlük açık = (1,0 × 7.700) ÷ 7 = 1.100 kcal
Günlük hedef: 2.900 − 1.100 = 1.800 kcal
Zaman çizelgesi: 15 hafta — ama kası korumak için yüksek protein ve direnç antrenmanı gerektirir
content.howItWorks_1: Mevcut kilonuzu, hedef kilonuzu ve TDEE'nizi girin (bilmiyorsanız TDEE Hesaplayıcımızı kullanın). Ardından hafif (0,25 kg/hafta) ile agresif (1,0 kg/hafta) arasında istediğiniz haftalık kilo verme oranını seçin. Hesaplayıcı bu girişleri kullanarak gerekli günlük kalori açığını belirler.
content.howItWorks_2: Hesapla'ya tıklayın. Formül, günlük açığı almak için haftalık kayıp hedefinizin enerji eşdeğerini 7'ye böler, ardından günlük kalori hedefinizi üretmek için TDEE'nizden çıkarır. Ayrıca gereken toplam açığı ve tahmini zaman çizelgenizi de hesaplar.
content.howItWorks_3: Günlük kalori hedefinizi öğün planlamanızın çıpası olarak kullanın. Herhangi bir kalori takip uygulamasıyla gıda alımınızı takip edin. Kendinizi haftalık tartın (aynı gün, aynı saatte, aç karnına) ve beklenen kayıp oranıyla karşılaştırın. Gerçek kayıp, beklentiden önemli ölçüde farklıysa, gerçek dünya verilerine dayanarak TDEE'yi yeniden hesaplayın.
content.whyItMatters:
The single biggest reason diets fail is not willpower — it is the absence of a rational, personalized calorie target. Generic diet plans prescribe arbitrary calorie levels that may be wildly inappropriate for a specific individual's metabolism, activity level, and body size. A 1,200 kcal plan appropriate for a 50 kg sedentary woman is dangerously restrictive for a 90 kg moderately active man whose TDEE is 2,800 kcal. Without calculating the right number for you specifically, weight loss becomes guesswork.

Calorie deficit science also explains why crash diets fail long-term. An extreme deficit (1,000+ kcal/day below TDEE) produces rapid initial weight loss but triggers metabolic adaptation — the body reduces BMR by 15–30%, increases hunger hormones (ghrelin rises, leptin falls), and decreases NEAT (unconscious movement). The result is that the effective deficit shrinks to near zero within weeks, weight loss stalls, and the elevated hunger makes the diet unsustainable. The research on this is unambiguous: moderate, consistent deficits of 300–600 kcal/day produce better long-term outcomes than aggressive restriction.

Understanding your specific deficit also allows you to plan with realistic timelines. Losing 15 kg takes approximately 30 weeks at a 500 kcal/day deficit — not 6 weeks, as many fad diets claim. Setting expectations based on real numbers prevents the frustration and abandonment that unrealistic timelines cause. Knowing your timeline lets you commit to a plan that actually works.
content.limitations:
The 7,700 kcal/kg equation assumes all weight lost comes from pure fat tissue. In reality, weight loss always includes some water loss, glycogen depletion, and — especially at aggressive deficits — lean mass (muscle and organ tissue). In the early weeks of a calorie deficit, significant water and glycogen loss often causes rapid scale weight reduction (2–5 kg in the first 1–2 weeks) that is not fat. Conversely, water retention from stress, sodium, menstrual cycle, and inflammation can mask real fat loss for 1–3 weeks.

The formula also cannot account for individual metabolic adaptation. Two people following identical deficits from the same starting TDEE can lose weight at meaningfully different rates due to genetic differences in metabolic efficiency, differences in actual NEAT response to restriction, and variation in food thermodynamics. This is why the calculated timeline is a projection, not a guarantee — reality will differ by ±20–30% for most individuals.

This calculator provides a calorie target only — it does not specify macronutrient distribution, meal timing, food quality, or other factors that substantially influence the composition of weight lost (fat vs muscle), dietary adherence, and health outcomes during restriction. Protein intake in particular is critical: at the same calorie deficit, high protein intake (1.6–2.4 g/kg) preserves significantly more lean mass than low protein intake.
content.tips_1: Choose a deficit rate you can sustain for the full duration. A 0.5 kg/week deficit maintained for 20 weeks produces better results than a 1.0 kg/week deficit abandoned after 6 weeks due to hunger and fatigue. Adherence over time is the most powerful predictor of fat loss success — more powerful than the specific deficit size.
content.tips_2: Prioritize protein at 1.6–2.2 g/kg body weight per day during your deficit. High protein intake is the single most powerful tool for preserving muscle mass during calorie restriction, increasing satiety (protein is the most filling macronutrient per calorie), and boosting metabolic rate through the thermic effect. If you hit your protein target within your calorie budget, fill remaining calories with carbohydrates and fat in proportions that suit your preferences.
content.tips_3: Include resistance training 2–4 times per week during your deficit. Strength training sends a strong signal to the body to preserve (or even build) muscle tissue even while in a calorie deficit. Without this signal, a portion of your weight loss will inevitably come from muscle, reducing metabolic rate and changing body composition unfavorably. You do not need to lift heavy — bodyweight training is sufficient to provide the muscle-preserving signal.
content.tips_4: Recalculate your TDEE and calorie target every 4–6 weeks as your weight changes. A 10 kg reduction in body weight typically reduces TDEE by 150–250 kcal/day. Failing to adjust your target downward as you lose weight is the most common reason fat loss plateaus — your original deficit gradually disappears as your smaller body requires fewer calories at the same intake level.
